1:
"And after five days Ananias the high priest descended with the elders, and with a certain orator named Tertullus, who informed the governor against Paul." The witnesses note the high priest and elders coming down to Caesarea to accuse Paul. Gill notes the five days are reckoned from Paul's coming to Caesarea; Ananias the high priest came down with the elders (members of the sanhedrim), not merely as accusers ordered by the captain, but willingly, to vindicate themselves and their people, lest they fall under the governor's displeasure for encouraging tumults; the high priest conscious he had acted illegally. The witnesses note the high priest, elders, and the hired orator Tertullus descending to Caesarea to prosecute Paul — the formal accusation, the leaders pursuing their enmity before the governor. The witnesses note the accusers' coming. The witnesses note the hired orator.

The witnesses note the high priest, elders, and orator Tertullus coming to Caesarea to accuse Paul — the leaders pursuing their enmity.

So the witnesses make the verse the accusers' coming to Caesarea. The lesson the expositors draw is the eagerness of the Jewish leaders to prosecute Paul, descending in person with a hired orator. The witnesses note the timing: "after five days" — Gill reckons the five days from Paul's coming to Caesarea; the leaders following quickly to press their accusation; the prompt pursuit of their enmity. The expositors observe the accusers: "Ananias the high priest descended with the elders, and with a certain orator named Tertullus" — the high priest, the elders (the sanhedrim), and a hired orator came down; Gill notes they came not merely as accusers ordered by the captain, but willingly, to vindicate themselves and their people, lest they fall under the governor's displeasure; the leaders pursuing in person. The witnesses press the eagerness of the leaders: the high priest and elders, the highest authorities, descended from Jerusalem to Caesarea to prosecute one prisoner; the depth of their enmity; the leaders stooping to pursue Paul; the malice of the great against the apostle. The expositors draw the lesson on the persistence of the enemies: the leaders would not let the matter rest, but followed Paul to Caesarea, hired an orator, pressed the accusation; the persistent malice; the enmity that pursues; the hatred that would not be satisfied. The witnesses observe the hiring of Tertullus: "a certain orator named Tertullus, who informed the governor against Paul" — the leaders hired a professional orator to plead their case; the skilled advocate engaged against the apostle; the eloquence of man arrayed against the truth; the hired tongue. The expositors note the self-interest of the leaders that Gill marks: the high priest, conscious he had acted illegally, came to vindicate himself and his people before the governor, lest they be charged with encouraging tumults; the self-interest mingled with the malice; the leaders covering their own fault. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the accusers' coming, the eagerness of the leaders, and the hiring of the orator against Paul. The verse thus records the high priest and elders coming to Caesarea — with the orator Tertullus — teaching the eagerness of the Jewish leaders to prosecute Paul and the eloquence of man arrayed against the truth.
2:
"And when he was called forth, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying, Seeing that by thee we enjoy great quietness, and that very worthy deeds are done unto this nation by thy providence," The witnesses note the flattering opening of Tertullus's speech. Barnes notes "we enjoy great quietness" was said in the customary style of flatterers and orators, to conciliate the governor's favour; strikingly in contrast with the more honest and straightforward introduction in Paul's reply (v.10); and though said for flattery, and though Felix was an unjust ruler, the orator opened with praise. The witnesses note Tertullus, called to accuse Paul, opening with flattery of Felix — praising his peace and good deeds — the orator's art of conciliating the judge by flattery; the contrast with Paul's honesty. The witnesses note the flattering exordium. The witnesses note the orator's art.

The witnesses note Tertullus opening with flattery of Felix — the orator's art of conciliating the judge, contrasted with Paul's honesty.

So the witnesses make the verse the flattering opening of Tertullus. The lesson the expositors draw is the orator's art of flattery to conciliate the judge, set in contrast with Paul's honest defence. The witnesses note the flattery: "by thee we enjoy great quietness, and that very worthy deeds are done unto this nation by thy providence" — Tertullus opens by praising Felix's peace and good government; Barnes notes this was said in the customary style of flatterers and orators, to conciliate the governor's favour; the captatio benevolentiae, the flattering exordium. The expositors observe the falseness of the flattery: Felix was in fact an unjust and cruel ruler (as v.25-27 show); the praise of his "quietness" and "worthy deeds" was flattery, not truth; the orator flattering the corrupt judge; the false praise. The witnesses press Barnes's contrast with Paul: the flattering opening is strikingly in contrast with the more honest and straightforward introduction in Paul's reply (v.10); Tertullus flatters, Paul speaks plainly; the difference between the orator's art and the apostle's honesty; the flattery and the truth. The expositors draw the lesson on the corruption of flattery: Tertullus seeks to win the judge by flattery rather than by the justice of his cause; the appeal to the judge's vanity; the corruption of pleading by flattery; the truth not needing such arts. The witnesses observe the orator's art: Tertullus, the hired orator, employs the arts of rhetoric — the flattering exordium, the conciliation of the judge; the eloquence of man, serving an unjust cause by flattery; the skilled tongue. The expositors note the contrast of the causes: the flattery covers the weakness of the case against Paul; the orator must win by art what he cannot win by justice; the flattery the cloak of a weak cause; the truth on Paul's side. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the flattering exordium, the orator's art, and the contrast with Paul's honest defence. The verse thus records the flattering opening of Tertullus — praising Felix's peace — teaching the orator's art of flattery to conciliate the judge, contrasted with the apostle's honest defence.
3:
"We accept it always, and in all places, most noble Felix, with all thankfulness." The witnesses note the continued flattery of Tertullus. Barnes paraphrases: "We admit that it is owing to your vigilance, and we accept your interposition to promote peace, with gratitude; what we now do in your presence, we do also in your absence; we do not commend you merely when you are present." Gill notes the Jews professed to acknowledge the favours and blessings they enjoyed under his government. The witnesses note Tertullus professing the nation's constant, universal gratitude to Felix — the lavish flattery of the orator, heaping praise to win the judge's favour before the accusation; the art of the hired tongue. The witnesses note the lavish flattery. The witnesses note the conciliation of the judge.

The witnesses note Tertullus professing constant gratitude to Felix — the lavish flattery heaped to win the judge's favour.

So the witnesses make the verse the continued flattery of Tertullus. The lesson the expositors draw is the lavish flattery of the orator, heaping praise to win the judge's favour. The witnesses note the profession of gratitude: "we accept it always, and in all places, most noble Felix, with all thankfulness" — Tertullus professes the nation's constant, universal gratitude to Felix; Barnes paraphrases — we accept your interposition with gratitude, always and everywhere, in your presence and absence; the lavish profession of thankfulness. The expositors observe the extravagance of the flattery: "always, and in all places... with all thankfulness" — the orator heaps the superlatives, professing constant, universal, hearty gratitude; the extravagant flattery; the praise piled high to win the judge. The witnesses press the falseness of the profession: the Jews did not in truth love Felix, who was cruel and unjust; the profession of constant gratitude was flattery, not truth; the false praise heaped to gain favour; the hollow profession. The expositors draw the lesson on the art of flattery: Tertullus seeks to win Felix's favour by extravagant praise before bringing the accusation; the conciliation of the judge; the art that flatters before it accuses; the orator's craft. The witnesses observe the title "most noble Felix": the respectful, flattering title; the address calculated to please; the courtesy of the orator serving his flattery; the honour given to win favour. The expositors note the contrast with Paul's brief courtesy: Paul, in his reply (v.10), gives Felix a brief, honest acknowledgment, not extravagant flattery; the difference between the orator's lavish flattery and the apostle's honest courtesy; the truth and the flattery contrasted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the lavish flattery, the conciliation of the judge, and the contrast with Paul's honest courtesy. The verse thus records the continued flattery of Tertullus — constant gratitude to Felix — teaching the lavish flattery of the orator, heaping praise to win the judge's favour.
4:
"Notwithstanding, that I be not further tedious unto thee, I pray thee that thou wouldest hear us of thy clemency a few words." The witnesses note the orator's transition to the accusation. Clarke notes Tertullus suggests he would not trespass longer on the governor's time, nor on his modesty by enumerating his beneficent deeds; and asks him to hear their complaint of his clemency; "the whole of this exordium was artful enough." Barnes notes he would not be tedious by taking up the time with an introduction and commendation. The witnesses note Tertullus, having flattered, turning to his accusation, asking Felix to hear briefly of his clemency — the artful transition, the flattery yielding to the charge; the orator's craft. The witnesses note the transition. The witnesses note the artful exordium.

The witnesses note Tertullus turning from flattery to the accusation — the artful transition, the orator's craft.

So the witnesses make the verse the orator's transition to the accusation. The lesson the expositors draw is the artful transition from flattery to the charge, the orator's craft. The witnesses note the transition: "that I be not further tedious unto thee, I pray thee that thou wouldest hear us of thy clemency a few words" — Tertullus, having flattered, now turns to his accusation, asking Felix to hear briefly; Barnes notes he would not be tedious by taking up the time with an introduction; the move from praise to charge. The expositors observe Clarke's note on the artfulness: Tertullus suggests he would not trespass longer on the governor's time, nor on his modesty by enumerating his deeds; and asks him to hear their complaint of his clemency; "the whole of this exordium was artful enough"; the craft of the orator. The witnesses press the orator's craft: the flattering exordium (v.2-3), the modest transition (v.4), the accusation to follow (v.5); the practiced structure of the rhetorician; the art that wins the judge before it accuses; the orator's skill. The expositors draw the lesson on the appeal to clemency: Tertullus appeals to Felix's "clemency" (kindness, patience) to hear them; the flattery continued in the very transition; the orator flattering even as he asks a hearing; the craft sustained. The witnesses observe the brevity professed: "a few words" — Tertullus professes brevity, that he may not weary the judge; the modest profession; the orator's art of seeming concise; the calculated brevity. The expositors note the contrast with the truth of the case: the orator's art and flattery cover the weakness of the case; the craft needed because the cause was unjust; the rhetoric serving a false accusation; the art and the falsehood. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the artful transition, the appeal to clemency, and the orator's craft. The verse thus records the orator's transition to the accusation — hear us of thy clemency — teaching the artful transition from flattery to the charge, the orator's craft serving an unjust cause.
5:
"For we have found this man a pestilent fellow, and a mover of sedition among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes:" The witnesses note the threefold charge against Paul. Gill notes "pestilent fellow" (the word signifies the pest or plague itself); it was usual with Roman orators to call a man they would represent as wicked the "pest" of the state. Barnes notes the word applied to one who corrupts the morals of others or excites sedition; Tertullus charged Paul as a plague, a mover of sedition everywhere, and a ringleader of the Nazarenes. The witnesses note the threefold accusation — pestilent fellow, mover of sedition, ringleader of the Nazarenes — the charges of the orator, framed to make Paul appear a danger to the state. The witnesses note the threefold charge. The witnesses note the political accusation.

The witnesses note the threefold charge — pestilent fellow, mover of sedition, ringleader of the Nazarenes — framed to make Paul a danger.

So the witnesses make the verse the threefold charge. The lesson the expositors draw is the political framing of the accusation, making Paul appear a danger to the state. The witnesses note the first charge: "a pestilent fellow" — Gill notes the word signifies the pest or plague itself; it was usual with Roman orators to call a wicked man the "pest" of the state; Barnes notes it applied to one who corrupts others or excites sedition; the apostle branded a plague. The expositors observe the second charge: "a mover of sedition among all the Jews throughout the world" — Tertullus charges Paul with stirring up sedition among the Jews everywhere; the political charge, calculated to alarm the Roman governor (for the Romans feared sedition); the apostle made a disturber of the peace. The witnesses press the third charge: "a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes" — Tertullus charges Paul as a leader of the Christians (contemptuously called "Nazarenes," after Jesus of Nazareth); the religious charge, making him the head of a despised sect; the apostle branded the ringleader. The expositors draw the lesson on the political framing: the charges were framed to make Paul appear a danger to the Roman state — a plague, a mover of sedition; the religious dispute dressed up as political sedition; the accusation calculated to alarm the governor; the framing of the charge. The witnesses observe the falseness of the charges: Paul was no mover of sedition (as his defence shows, v.12); the charges were false, the framing malicious; the apostle slandered as a seditious plague; the false accusation. The expositors note the contempt in "Nazarenes": the Christians called "Nazarenes" in contempt, after the despised town of Nazareth ("can any good thing come out of Nazareth?" John 1:46); the scorn in the term; Paul (v.14) does not stoop to dispute the name; the contempt borne. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the threefold charge, the political framing, and the false accusation against Paul. The verse thus records the threefold charge — pestilent fellow, mover of sedition, ringleader of the Nazarenes — teaching the political framing of the accusation, making Paul appear a danger to the state.
6:
"Who also hath gone about to profane the temple: whom we took, and would have judged according to our law." The witnesses note the charge of profaning the temple. Gill notes the charge of profaning the temple by introducing a Greek into it was only a supposition and conjecture of the Asiatic Jews, a false and groundless one. Barnes notes this was a serious but unfounded charge, arising from the calumny that he had introduced Greeks into the sacred place (Acts 21:28), to which Paul replies in v.18; and "would have judged" means they would have condemned and punished him according to their law. The witnesses note the charge that Paul profaned the temple, and the claim that they would have judged him lawfully — the unfounded charge and the false claim of lawful intent. The witnesses note the temple charge. The witnesses note the false claim of lawful judgment.

The witnesses note the unfounded charge of profaning the temple and the false claim of lawful judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the charge of profaning the temple. The lesson the expositors draw is the unfounded nature of the charge and the false claim that they would have judged Paul lawfully. The witnesses note the charge: "who also hath gone about to profane the temple" — Tertullus charges Paul with attempting to profane the temple; Gill notes the charge (of introducing a Greek into the temple) was only a supposition and conjecture of the Asiatic Jews, false and groundless; Barnes notes it arose from the calumny that he had brought Greeks into the sacred place (Acts 21:28). The expositors observe the falseness of the charge: the charge was unfounded — Paul had not profaned the temple, but was purifying himself there (v.18); the accusation a calumny, based on the false supposition of the Asian Jews; the unfounded charge; Paul replies to it in v.18. The witnesses press the claim of lawful intent: "whom we took, and would have judged according to our law" — Tertullus claims they seized Paul intending to judge him lawfully, according to their law; the claim of orderly, lawful process. The expositors draw the lesson on the falseness of this claim: in truth, the Jews had not intended to judge Paul lawfully, but to kill him (Acts 21:31; 23:12); the claim of lawful intent was false; they would have murdered him, not tried him; the false pretence of legality covering the murderous intent. The witnesses observe the distortion of the facts: Tertullus distorts the events — the unlawful mob violence dressed up as a lawful seizure and intended trial; the facts twisted to favour the accusers; the orator's distortion. The expositors note the appeal to "our law": Tertullus appeals to the Jewish law (which forbade strangers in the temple) as the ground of their action; the religious law invoked; the charge framed as a violation of their sacred law; the appeal to the law they themselves had not kept. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unfounded temple charge, the false claim of lawful judgment, and the distortion of the facts. The verse thus records the charge of profaning the temple — and the claim of lawful judgment — teaching the unfounded nature of the charge and the false pretence of legality covering the murderous intent.
7:
"But the chief captain Lysias came upon us, and with great violence took him away out of our hands," The witnesses note Tertullus's complaint against Lysias. Barnes notes Tertullus pretends they would have judged Paul righteously had Lysias not interposed; "but the truth was, that, without regard to law or justice, they would have murdered him on the spot." Clarke notes "with great violence" rather means with an armed force; Tertullus intimates that Lysias interfered contrary to law. The witnesses note Tertullus complaining that Lysias took Paul from them by force — the distortion that blamed the captain for preventing a lawful trial, when in truth he prevented a murder; the orator's inversion of the facts. The witnesses note the complaint against Lysias. The witnesses note the inverted facts.

The witnesses note Tertullus blaming Lysias for taking Paul — inverting the facts, for the captain prevented a murder, not a trial.

So the witnesses make the verse the complaint against Lysias. The lesson the expositors draw is the inversion of the facts, blaming the captain who in truth prevented a murder. The witnesses note the complaint: "the chief captain Lysias came upon us, and with great violence took him away out of our hands" — Tertullus complains that Lysias seized Paul from them by force; Clarke notes "with great violence" rather means with an armed force; the charge against the captain. The expositors observe Barnes's exposure of the pretence: Tertullus pretends they would have judged Paul righteously had Lysias not interposed; but the truth was, without regard to law or justice, they would have murdered him on the spot (Acts 21:31); the false pretence; the captain blamed for preventing a murder. The witnesses press the inversion of the facts: Tertullus inverts the truth — the captain, who rescued Paul from the murderous mob (Acts 21:32), is blamed as an unlawful interferer; the deliverer made the offender; the facts turned upside down; the orator's distortion. The expositors draw the lesson on the slander of Lysias: Tertullus slanders the captain — charging him with violence and unlawful interference, when in truth he acted to preserve a Roman citizen from being murdered; the just deliverer maligned; the slander of the righteous act. The witnesses observe the boldness of the distortion: Tertullus distorts events that Lysias himself could refute (and had reported, Acts 23:26-30); the bold inversion, hoping the governor would not learn the truth; the orator's audacity; the distortion risked. The expositors note the danger of the inversion: by blaming Lysias, Tertullus sought to remove the inconvenient witness whose report would clear Paul; the attempt to discredit the captain; the orator undermining the truth; the inversion serving the false case. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the complaint against Lysias, the inversion of the facts, and the slander of the captain who prevented a murder. The verse thus records Tertullus's complaint against Lysias — taking Paul by force — teaching the inversion of the facts, blaming the captain who in truth prevented a murder.
8:
"Commanding his accusers to come unto thee: by examining of whom thyself mayest take knowledge of all these things, whereof we accuse him." The witnesses note Tertullus's appeal to the governor's examination. Gill notes Tertullus would insinuate that the captain was blameworthy for hindering a legal process, and that it was owing to him the governor was troubled. Barnes notes the appeal — by examining the accusers (or Paul), Felix could learn the truth of the charges. The witnesses note Tertullus directing Felix to examine and learn the charges — the orator resting his case, confident (falsely) that examination would confirm the accusation; the appeal to the judge's inquiry. The witnesses note the appeal to examination. The witnesses note the resting of the case.

The witnesses note Tertullus appealing to Felix's examination to confirm the charges — resting the case on the judge's inquiry.

So the witnesses make the verse Tertullus's appeal to the governor's examination. The lesson the expositors draw is the orator resting his case on the judge's inquiry, falsely confident the examination would confirm the charge. The witnesses note the appeal: "by examining of whom thyself mayest take knowledge of all these things, whereof we accuse him" — Tertullus directs Felix to examine (the accusers, or Paul) and so learn the truth of the charges; Barnes notes the appeal to the governor's inquiry; the case rested on examination. The expositors observe the implication: Tertullus implies that examination would confirm the accusation; the orator confident (falsely) that the inquiry would establish the guilt; the case rested on the judge's investigation; the appeal to examination. The witnesses press the falseness of the confidence: in truth, examination would clear Paul (as his defence and the absent witnesses show, v.13, 19); the orator's confidence misplaced; the examination would not confirm but refute the charge; the false confidence. The expositors draw the lesson on the weakness of the case: Tertullus rests on examination, but the examination would expose the falsehood; the weak case that could not bear inquiry; the accusation that examination would overthrow; the truth that inquiry would reveal. The witnesses observe Gill's note on the insinuation against Lysias: Tertullus would insinuate that the captain was blameworthy for hindering a legal process and troubling the governor; the continued attack on Lysias; the orator shifting blame; the insinuation against the captain. The expositors note the irony of the appeal: Tertullus invites examination, confident of confirming the charge; yet the examination (Paul's defence, the absent witnesses) would clear Paul; the appeal that backfired; the inquiry favouring the truth; the irony of the invitation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the appeal to examination, the resting of the case, and the false confidence that inquiry would confirm the charge. The verse thus records Tertullus's appeal to the governor's examination — that Felix might learn the charges — teaching the orator resting his case on the judge's inquiry, falsely confident the examination would confirm the false charge.
9:
"And the Jews also assented, saying that these things were so." The witnesses note the Jews' confirmation of the accusation. Gill notes Ananias the high priest and the elders agreed to what Tertullus said and confirmed it. Barnes notes the Jews who had accompanied Tertullus bore testimony, when called upon, confirming unanimously the accusation. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "joined in the charge" (sunepethento) — a double compound, to make a joint attack. The witnesses note the Jews joining in the accusation, confirming Tertullus's charges — the united front of the accusers, the leaders lending their assent to the orator's distortions; the weight of numbers against the truth. The witnesses note the Jews' assent. The witnesses note the united accusation.

The witnesses note the Jews confirming Tertullus's charges — the united front of the accusers, numbers against the truth.

So the witnesses make the verse the Jews' confirmation of the accusation. The lesson the expositors draw is the united front of the accusers, the weight of numbers lent to the orator's distortions. The witnesses note the assent: "the Jews also assented, saying that these things were so" — the Jews (Ananias and the elders) agreed to Tertullus's charges and confirmed them; Gill notes they agreed and confirmed; Robertson notes "joined in the charge" (a double compound, to make a joint attack); the united confirmation. The expositors observe the weight added: Barnes notes the Jews who had accompanied Tertullus bore testimony, confirming unanimously the accusation; the leaders lending their assent and testimony; the weight of numbers and authority added to the charge; the united front. The witnesses press the united attack: the high priest and elders, with the orator, made a joint attack on Paul; the combined force of the leaders against one prisoner; the weight of numbers and authority arrayed against the truth; the concerted assault. The expositors draw the lesson on the danger of united falsehood: the leaders united to confirm the false charges; the weight of numbers and authority lent to the distortions; the truth opposed by the united front; "they gathered themselves together against the soul of the righteous" (Psalm 94:21); the concerted enmity. The witnesses observe the assenting to known falsehood: the leaders assented to charges they knew to be distorted (the murderous intent, the false temple charge); the deliberate confirmation of falsehood; the leaders bearing false witness; the assent to the lie. The expositors note the providence overruling the united attack: though the leaders united against Paul, the providence would clear him (Felix deferring, v.22; the absent witnesses, v.19); the united falsehood not prevailing; the truth preserved; the providence over the assault. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Jews' assent, the united front of the accusers, and the weight of numbers against the truth. The verse thus records the Jews' confirmation of the accusation — saying these things were so — teaching the united front of the accusers and the weight of numbers lent to the orator's distortions.
10:
"Then Paul, after that the governor had beckoned unto him to speak, answered, Forasmuch as I know that thou hast been of many years a judge unto this nation, I do the more cheerfully answer for myself:" The witnesses note Paul's honest and dignified opening. Matthew Henry notes Paul's defence shows "a great deal of the spirit of wisdom and holiness, and an accomplishment of Christ's promise" that his followers, before governors, should be given what to speak; though Tertullus had said provoking things, Paul did not interrupt him. The witnesses note Paul, given leave to speak, opening not with flattery but with a brief, honest acknowledgment of Felix's long experience as judge — the truthful opening, the wisdom given by Christ, contrasted with the orator's flattery. The witnesses note the honest opening. The witnesses note the spirit of wisdom.

The witnesses note Paul's honest, dignified opening — a brief truthful acknowledgment, not flattery; the wisdom given by Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's honest opening. The lesson the expositors draw is the spirit of wisdom in Paul's defence, the truthful opening contrasted with the orator's flattery. The witnesses note the leave to speak: "after that the governor had beckoned unto him to speak" — Paul waited for the governor's signal before speaking; the order and courtesy of the apostle; the patient, respectful manner. The expositors observe the honest acknowledgment: "Forasmuch as I know that thou hast been of many years a judge unto this nation, I do the more cheerfully answer for myself" — Paul opens not with flattery, but with a brief, truthful acknowledgment of Felix's long experience as judge; the honest opening; the truth, not extravagant praise. The witnesses press the contrast with Tertullus: where the orator flattered (v.2-4), Paul speaks plainly; he notes only the true fact of Felix's experience (which gave ground for confidence), not false praise of his virtue; the honest courtesy contrasted with the flattery; the apostle's truthfulness. The expositors draw the lesson on the spirit of wisdom that Matthew Henry marks: Paul's defence shows the spirit of wisdom and holiness, and the fulfilment of Christ's promise (Matthew 10:19-20) that his followers, before governors, should be given what to speak; the wisdom given by the Spirit; the apostle's defence inspired. The witnesses observe the patient hearing Paul gave Tertullus: Matthew Henry notes that though Tertullus said provoking things, Paul did not interrupt him, but let him finish; the patience and courtesy of the apostle; the meekness that hears before it answers; the self-control. The expositors note the cheerful confidence: "I do the more cheerfully answer for myself" — Paul answers cheerfully, confident in his innocence and in Felix's experience to judge; the calm confidence of the innocent; the cheerful defence; the apostle at peace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's honest opening, the spirit of wisdom, and the contrast with the orator's flattery. The verse thus records Paul's honest opening — a truthful acknowledgment, not flattery — teaching the spirit of wisdom in his defence and the truthfulness contrasted with the orator's flattery.
11:
"Because that thou mayest understand, that there are yet but twelve days since I went up to Jerusalem for to worship." The witnesses note Paul's appeal to the brevity of his time in Jerusalem. Barnes notes Felix could ascertain by proper testimony that Paul had been but twelve days in Jerusalem, and had been orderly and peaceable; the first part of Paul's defence (v.11-13) consists in an express denial of what they alleged. The witnesses note Paul pointing out that he had been only twelve days in Jerusalem, and came to worship — too short a time, and too pious a purpose, to have stirred the sedition charged; the appeal to the verifiable facts. The witnesses note the twelve days. The witnesses note the purpose to worship.

The witnesses note Paul's appeal to the twelve days and his purpose to worship — too short a time, too pious a purpose, for sedition.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's appeal to the brevity of his time. The lesson the expositors draw is the appeal to the verifiable facts, refuting the charge of sedition. The witnesses note the brevity: "there are yet but twelve days since I went up to Jerusalem" — Paul points out he had been only twelve days in Jerusalem; Barnes notes Felix could ascertain by proper testimony that Paul had been but twelve days there, and had been orderly; the short time, easily verified. The expositors observe the purpose: "for to worship" — Paul came to Jerusalem to worship, a pious purpose; not to stir sedition, but to worship God; the devout intent; the purpose refuting the charge. The witnesses press the force of the appeal: twelve days, and that to worship — too short a time, and too pious a purpose, to have raised the sedition "among all the Jews throughout the world" charged (v.5); the verifiable facts refuting the wild accusation; the appeal to fact. The expositors draw the lesson on the express denial, which Barnes marks: the first part of Paul's defence (v.11-13) is an express denial of the charges; Paul meets the accusation with facts — the short time, the pious purpose, the absence of any tumult; the plain refutation; the denial grounded in fact. The witnesses observe the appeal to verifiable testimony: Paul invites Felix to verify the facts — the twelve days, the peaceable conduct; the appeal to evidence, not rhetoric; the apostle resting on the truth; the verifiable defence. The expositors note the contrast with the orator's distortions: where Tertullus distorted and exaggerated (sedition "throughout the world"), Paul appeals to the plain, verifiable facts; the truth against the distortion; the fact against the flourish; the honest defence. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the twelve days, the purpose to worship, and the appeal to the verifiable facts. The verse thus records Paul's appeal to the twelve days and his purpose to worship — teaching the appeal to the verifiable facts, refuting the charge of sedition.
12:
"And they neither found me in the temple disputing with any man, neither raising up the people, neither in the synagogues, nor in the city:" The witnesses note Paul's denial of any disturbance. Gill notes Paul was so far from moving sedition that he never even entered into conversation with the people on any subject; true, he was found in the temple, but not disputing, but purifying himself according to the law. Barnes notes this meets the first charge of Tertullus. The witnesses note Paul denying that he was found disputing or stirring up the people anywhere — in the temple, the synagogues, or the city — the plain denial of the sedition charged; no disturbance found. The witnesses note the denial of disturbance. The witnesses note the peaceable conduct.

The witnesses note Paul denying any disturbance — not disputing or stirring the people anywhere; the plain denial of sedition.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's denial of any disturbance. The lesson the expositors draw is the plain denial of the sedition charged, the peaceable conduct of the apostle. The witnesses note the comprehensive denial: "they neither found me in the temple disputing with any man, neither raising up the people, neither in the synagogues, nor in the city" — Paul denies that he was found disputing or stirring up the people anywhere — not in the temple, the synagogues, or the city; the comprehensive denial; no disturbance found in any place. The expositors observe Gill's note on the peaceable conduct: Paul was so far from moving sedition that he never even entered into conversation with the people on any subject; true, he was found in the temple, but not disputing — purifying himself according to the law; the peaceable, quiet conduct; the absence of any tumult. The witnesses press the plain refutation: the charge was sedition (v.5); Paul answers that he was found making no disturbance — not disputing, not raising the people; the plain denial; the charge refuted by the absence of any act. The expositors draw the lesson on the innocence of the apostle: Paul's conduct gave no ground for the charge — he was peaceable, quiet, worshipping; the innocence shown in the absence of any disturbance; the blameless conduct; the charge groundless. The witnesses observe the challenge to the accusers: "they neither found me" — Paul challenges the accusers to produce any instance of his disturbing the peace; they found him in no such act; the challenge to evidence; the accusers unable to point to any disturbance. The expositors note the contrast with the charge: Tertullus charged a mover of sedition "throughout the world"; Paul answers that he was found making no disturbance even in Jerusalem; the wild charge against the quiet fact; the exaggeration against the truth; the innocence plain. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the denial of disturbance, the peaceable conduct, and the challenge to the accusers. The verse thus records Paul's denial of any disturbance — not disputing or stirring the people — teaching the plain denial of the sedition charged and the peaceable conduct of the apostle.
13:
"Neither can they prove the things whereof they now accuse me." The witnesses note Paul's challenge that the accusers cannot prove their charges. Gill notes that the charges — a pestilent fellow, a mover of sedition, a profaner of the temple — they could not set forth in any clear light, nor bring testimonies to demonstrate; they could not make them appear true, which it lay upon them to do, or he ought to be acquitted; "this was challenging and defying them to make good their assertions." Barnes notes Paul boldly challenges investigation. The witnesses note Paul defying the accusers to prove their charges — the bold challenge of the innocent, resting on the want of any proof; the burden on the accusers unmet. The witnesses note the challenge to prove. The witnesses note the want of proof.

The witnesses note Paul defying the accusers to prove their charges — the bold challenge of the innocent, the burden unmet.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's challenge to the accusers. The lesson the expositors draw is the bold challenge of the innocent, resting on the want of any proof. The witnesses note the challenge: "neither can they prove the things whereof they now accuse me" — Paul challenges the accusers to prove their charges; Gill notes they could not set forth the charges in any clear light, nor bring testimonies to demonstrate them; the defiance to prove. The expositors observe the burden on the accusers: it lay upon the accusers to prove their charges, or Paul ought to be acquitted; Gill notes "this was challenging and defying them to make good their assertions"; the burden of proof unmet; the accusers unable to demonstrate the charge. The witnesses press the boldness of the challenge: Barnes notes Paul boldly challenges investigation; the apostle, confident in his innocence, defies the accusers to prove anything; the bold challenge; the innocent unafraid of inquiry; the defiance of the truth. The expositors draw the lesson on the innocence resting on the want of proof: Paul's defence rests on the simple fact that the accusers could prove nothing; the charges unproven, the accusation falls; the innocence vindicated by the absence of proof; "if they could prove nothing, the charge must fall." The witnesses observe the contrast with the orator's flourish: Tertullus's rhetoric and flattery could not supply the want of proof; the orator's art against the want of evidence; the flourish against the fact; the rhetoric exposed by the demand for proof. The expositors note the principle of justice invoked: Paul invokes the principle that the accuser must prove the charge; the accused not to be condemned without proof; the just demand for evidence; the principle of justice on Paul's side. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's challenge to prove, the want of proof, and the bold confidence of the innocent. The verse thus records Paul's challenge to the accusers — neither can they prove the charges — teaching the bold challenge of the innocent, resting on the want of any proof.
14:
"But this I confess unto thee, that after the way which they call heresy, so worship I the God of my fathers, believing all things which are written in the law and in the prophets:" The witnesses note Paul's confession of the Way and its continuity with the faith of Israel. Barnes notes Paul does not stoop to the contempt in the word "Nazarenes," being engaged in more important business; he admits he belonged to that "way" (sect), neither denying nor disposed to deny he was a Christian; and maintains the continuity. The witnesses note Paul confessing that "after the way which they call heresy" (sect) he worships the God of his fathers, believing all written in the law and the prophets — owning the gospel openly, and affirming its continuity with the faith of Israel; Christianity not a new religion but the fulfilment. The witnesses note the confession of the Way. The witnesses note the continuity with the OT.

The witnesses note Paul confessing the Way and its continuity with Israel's faith — Christianity the fulfilment, not a new religion.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's confession of the Way. The lesson the expositors draw is the open confession of the gospel and its continuity with the faith of Israel. The witnesses note the confession: "this I confess unto thee, that after the way which they call heresy, so worship I the God of my fathers" — Paul openly confesses that he worships God "after the way" (the gospel) which his accusers call "heresy" (a sect); Barnes notes he admits he belonged to that way, neither denying nor disposed to deny he was a Christian; the open confession; the gospel owned. The expositors observe the dignity in not disputing the name: Barnes notes Paul does not stoop to the contempt in the word "Nazarenes" (v.5), being engaged in more important business than to contend about a name; the apostle above the scorn; the name borne without dispute; the dignity of the confession. The witnesses press the continuity with Israel's faith: "so worship I the God of my fathers, believing all things which are written in the law and in the prophets" — Paul affirms that in the Way he worships the same God of the fathers, believing all written in the law and the prophets; Christianity not a new religion, nor a departure from Israel's faith, but its fulfilment; the continuity affirmed. The expositors draw the lesson on the continuity of the gospel with the Old Testament: Paul worships the God of the fathers, believes the law and the prophets; the gospel the fulfilment of the Scriptures, not their contradiction; the Way the true Judaism, believing all the Scriptures (which pointed to Christ); the continuity; "to him give all the prophets witness" (Acts 10:43). The witnesses observe the answer to the charge of "heresy": the Jews called the Way "heresy" (a sect, a departure); Paul answers that it is the true worship of the God of the fathers, believing all the Scriptures; the charge refuted; the Way no heresy but the fulfilment; the true faith. The expositors note the boldness of the confession: Paul confesses the gospel openly before the governor, not ashamed; the bold owning of the Way; "I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ" (Romans 1:16); the courageous confession. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's confession of the Way, the continuity with Israel's faith, and the gospel as the fulfilment. The verse thus records Paul's confession of the Way — worshipping the God of the fathers, believing the law and the prophets — teaching the open confession of the gospel and its continuity with the faith of Israel.
15:
"And have hope toward God, which they themselves also allow, that there shall be a resurrection of the dead, both of the just and unjust." The witnesses note Paul's profession of the hope of the resurrection. Barnes notes Paul's hope of the resurrection arises from the promises of God; "which they themselves also allow" — that is, the Pharisees, who held this same cardinal point; as they held it, they could not arraign him for holding it; the resurrection of "both the just and unjust," of all the race. Gill notes the hope of an interest in God and eternal life. The witnesses note Paul professing the hope of the resurrection of the just and unjust — a hope the Pharisees among his accusers shared; the common ground that exposed the division among them and vindicated his orthodoxy. The witnesses note the hope of the resurrection. The witnesses note the common ground.

The witnesses note Paul professing the resurrection hope — shared by the Pharisees; the common ground vindicating his orthodoxy.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's profession of the resurrection hope. The lesson the expositors draw is the resurrection of the just and unjust, a hope shared by the Pharisees, vindicating Paul's orthodoxy. The witnesses note the hope: "have hope toward God... that there shall be a resurrection of the dead, both of the just and unjust" — Paul professes the hope of the resurrection of all, the just and the unjust; Barnes notes the hope arises from the promises of God; the resurrection of all the race; the great hope. The expositors observe the common ground: "which they themselves also allow" — Barnes notes the Pharisees held this same cardinal point; as they held it, they could not arraign Paul for holding it; the resurrection a hope shared with the Pharisees among his accusers; the common ground. The witnesses press the vindication of his orthodoxy: Paul's hope of the resurrection was the orthodox Jewish hope (held by the Pharisees); in professing it, Paul showed his faith was not heresy but the true Jewish hope; the orthodoxy vindicated; the Way holding the orthodox hope. The expositors draw the lesson on the resurrection of the just and unjust: Paul professes the resurrection of all — the just to life, the unjust to judgment; the universal resurrection; "they that have done good, unto the resurrection of life; and they that have done evil, unto the resurrection of damnation" (John 5:29); the hope and the warning. The witnesses observe the wisdom of appealing to the common hope: by professing the resurrection, Paul appealed to the hope the Pharisees shared, exposing the division between Pharisees and Sadducees (as Acts 23:6-9), and vindicating his orthodoxy; the wise appeal; the common ground turned to his defence. The expositors note the hope toward God: Paul's hope was "toward God" — grounded in God's promises, resting on him; the hope of the believer; the confidence in God's faithfulness; the hope that sustains. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's hope of the resurrection, the common ground with the Pharisees, and the vindication of his orthodoxy. The verse thus records Paul's profession of the resurrection hope — of the just and unjust — teaching the resurrection shared by the Pharisees, vindicating Paul's orthodoxy.
16:
"And herein do I exercise myself, to have always a conscience void of offence toward God, and toward men." The witnesses note Paul's exercise of a good conscience. Gill notes that because of this doctrine of the resurrection (which promotes a holy life), Paul studied, laboured, and bent himself, employing his thought, care, and time. Barnes notes "I exercise myself" (askō) means "I make it my constant aim and endeavour"; Paul often appeals to his conscientiousness as the leading habit of his life. The witnesses note Paul professing that he labours to keep a conscience void of offence toward God and men — the fruit of the resurrection hope in a holy, conscientious life; the good conscience the aim of the believer. The witnesses note the good conscience. The witnesses note the conscientious life.

The witnesses note Paul's exercise of a good conscience toward God and men — the fruit of the resurrection hope in a holy life.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's exercise of a good conscience. The lesson the expositors draw is the holy, conscientious life as the fruit of the resurrection hope. The witnesses note the exercise: "herein do I exercise myself, to have always a conscience void of offence" — Paul professes that he labours to keep a good conscience; Barnes notes "I exercise myself" (askō) means he makes it his constant aim and endeavour; the diligent pursuit of a good conscience; the labour of the conscientious life. The expositors observe the connection with the resurrection hope: Gill notes that because of the doctrine of the resurrection (which promotes a holy life), Paul studied and laboured to keep a good conscience; the resurrection hope producing the holy life; the doctrine according to godliness; the hope and the practice joined. The witnesses press the scope of the conscience: "toward God, and toward men" — Paul labours for a conscience void of offence both toward God (in piety, worship, faith) and toward men (in justice, honesty, love); the conscience clear in both directions; the duty to God and neighbour; the whole of the law. The expositors draw the lesson on the good conscience as the aim of the believer: Paul makes it his constant aim to keep a conscience void of offence; the good conscience the mark of the holy life; "a good conscience" (1 Timothy 1:5, 19); the conscientious walk; the aim of the believer. The witnesses observe the appeal to his conscientiousness: Barnes notes Paul often appeals to his conscientiousness as the leading habit of his life; the conscience void of offence his constant pursuit; the appeal to his integrity; the conscientious character. The expositors note the contrast with the charge: Paul, charged as a pestilent mover of sedition (v.5), professes a life of conscientious integrity toward God and men; the holy life against the slander; the good conscience refuting the charge; the integrity vindicating. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's exercise of a good conscience, the fruit of the resurrection hope, and the conscientious life toward God and men. The verse thus records Paul's exercise of a good conscience — void of offence toward God and men — teaching the holy, conscientious life as the fruit of the resurrection hope.
17:
"Now after many years I came to bring alms to my nation, and offerings." The witnesses note Paul's account of the purpose of his coming. Barnes notes Paul here begins to reply to the charge of profaning the temple; his design in coming to Jerusalem was to bring needed aid in a time of distress (the collection, Romans 15:25-26); it would be absurd to suppose his object was to violate the customs of the temple. Gill notes it was now about twenty-five years since his conversion, most spent among the Gentiles. The witnesses note Paul explaining that he came, after many years, to bring alms and offerings to his nation — a purpose of charity and piety, refuting the charge that he came to profane the temple. The witnesses note the alms and offerings. The witnesses note the charitable purpose.

The witnesses note Paul's purpose to bring alms and offerings — a charitable, pious aim, refuting the charge of profaning the temple.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's account of his charitable purpose. The lesson the expositors draw is the charitable, pious purpose of Paul's coming, refuting the charge of profaning the temple. The witnesses note the purpose: "after many years I came to bring alms to my nation, and offerings" — Paul explains that he came to Jerusalem to bring alms (the collection for the poor saints) and offerings; Barnes notes his design was to bring needed aid in a time of distress (Romans 15:25-26); the charitable purpose. The expositors observe the refutation of the temple charge: Barnes notes it would be absurd to suppose that one who came to bring alms and offerings came to violate the customs of the temple; the charitable, pious purpose refuting the charge of profaning; the design clearing the accusation; the purpose against the charge. The witnesses press the charity of the purpose: Paul came "to bring alms to my nation" — the collection from the Gentile churches for the poor saints at Jerusalem; the labour of love; the apostle bringing relief to his nation; the charity that refutes the charge of enmity. The expositors draw the lesson on the love behind the collection: Paul, charged as an enemy of his nation, came bringing alms to them; the love that returns good; the collection a labour of love, knitting the Gentile and Jewish believers; the charity that proves his goodwill; the love against the slander. The witnesses observe the piety of the offerings: Paul came also to bring "offerings" — to worship, to fulfil his vow (v.18); the pious purpose; the apostle worshipping in the temple, not profaning it; the piety refuting the charge. The expositors note the long absence: Gill notes it was about twenty-five years since Paul's conversion, most spent among the Gentiles; "after many years" he returned to Jerusalem; the apostle's long labour among the Gentiles, now returning with their gift; the history behind the visit. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's charitable purpose, the alms and offerings, and the refutation of the temple charge. The verse thus records Paul's purpose to bring alms and offerings — teaching the charitable, pious purpose of his coming, refuting the charge of profaning the temple.
18:
"Whereupon certain Jews from Asia found me purified in the temple, neither with multitude, nor with tumult." The witnesses note Paul's account of how he was found in the temple. Clarke notes the Jews of Asia, who stirred up the persecution, found Paul purified in the temple, regularly performing his religious vow, giving no cause for suspicion — making no tumult, nor having a number of people with him by whom he could have accomplished a seditious purpose. Barnes notes he was found engaged in the sacred service of completing his vow, in a quiet and peaceful manner. The witnesses note Paul stating that the Asian Jews found him purified in the temple, quietly fulfilling his vow, without crowd or tumult — the very opposite of the sedition charged; the facts refuting the accusation. The witnesses note the purification. The witnesses note the absence of tumult.

The witnesses note Paul found purified in the temple, without crowd or tumult — the opposite of the sedition charged.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's account of how he was found in the temple. The lesson the expositors draw is the peaceable, devout conduct of Paul, the opposite of the sedition charged. The witnesses note the purification: "certain Jews from Asia found me purified in the temple" — the Asian Jews found Paul purified in the temple, fulfilling his vow; Barnes notes he was engaged in the sacred service of completing his vow; the devout act; the apostle worshipping, not profaning. The expositors observe Clarke's note on the peaceable manner: the Asian Jews found Paul regularly performing his religious vow, giving no cause for suspicion — making no tumult, nor having a number of people with him by whom he could have accomplished a seditious purpose; the quiet, peaceable conduct; the absence of any seditious gathering. The witnesses press the absence of tumult: "neither with multitude, nor with tumult" — Paul was found alone, quietly fulfilling his vow, without crowd or tumult; the very opposite of the sedition charged; no multitude, no uproar; the facts refuting the accusation. The expositors draw the lesson on the peaceable conduct refuting the charge: Paul, charged as a mover of sedition and a profaner of the temple, was found quietly worshipping, fulfilling his vow, without crowd or tumult; the devout, peaceable conduct against the charge; the facts against the accusation; the innocence plain. The witnesses observe the irony: the very Jews who charged Paul with profaning the temple found him purifying himself in it; the accusers' own observation refuting their charge; the irony of the accusation; the charge refuted by the accusers' knowledge. The expositors note that the tumult was raised by the accusers, not Paul: the multitude and tumult (Acts 21:27-30) were raised by the Asian Jews, not by Paul; the accusers themselves the cause of the disturbance; the tumult charged on Paul was their own doing; the inversion exposed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the purification, the absence of tumult, and the peaceable conduct refuting the charge. The verse thus records Paul found purified in the temple, without crowd or tumult — teaching the peaceable, devout conduct of Paul, the opposite of the sedition charged.
19:
"Who ought to have been here before thee, and object, if they had ought against me." The witnesses note Paul's point that the proper witnesses were absent. Barnes notes the Asian Jews were the proper witnesses (they alone saw him in the temple); as they had stayed away, it showed they were not prepared to undergo a strict examination; Paul justly complains that the very persons who alone could testify against him were absent, showing there was no well-founded charge; they alone could testify as to what occurred in the temple, and being absent, the charge ought to be dismissed. The witnesses note Paul pointing out that the Asian Jews, who raised the outcry, ought to be present to accuse him, but were absent — the fatal flaw in the prosecution, the true witnesses missing. The witnesses note the absent witnesses. The witnesses note the flaw in the prosecution.

The witnesses note Paul pointing to the absent Asian Jews — the true witnesses missing, the fatal flaw in the prosecution.

So the witnesses make the verse the absence of the proper witnesses. The lesson the expositors draw is the fatal flaw in the prosecution, the true witnesses being absent. The witnesses note the point: "who ought to have been here before thee, and object, if they had ought against me" — Paul points out that the Asian Jews, who raised the outcry against him in the temple, ought to be present to accuse him, but were absent; the true witnesses missing. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the proper witnesses: the Asian Jews were the proper witnesses — they alone saw Paul in the temple and raised the charge; as they had stayed away, it showed they were not prepared to undergo a strict examination; the absence telling against the charge. The witnesses press the flaw in the prosecution: the very persons who alone could testify to what occurred in the temple were absent; the prosecution lacked its essential witnesses; the charge could not be proved without them; the fatal flaw; the case incomplete. The expositors draw the lesson on the dismissal the absence required: Barnes notes that, the proper witnesses being absent, the charge ought to be dismissed; the accusation could not stand without the witnesses; the law requiring the accusers to appear; the absence demanding acquittal. The witnesses observe the significance of the absence: the Asian Jews stayed away — perhaps because they could not prove the charge, or feared examination; the absence itself a confession of the weakness of the case; the missing accusers telling against the prosecution; the silence of the witnesses. The expositors note the justice of Paul's complaint: Paul justly complains that those who alone could accuse him were absent, while others (who had not seen the temple events) pressed the charge on hearsay; the injustice of being accused by those who could not testify; the just demand for the proper witnesses; the principle of justice. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the absent witnesses, the flaw in the prosecution, and the dismissal the absence required. The verse thus records the absence of the proper witnesses — who ought to have been here — teaching the fatal flaw in the prosecution, the true witnesses being absent.
20:
"Or else let these same here say, if they have found any evil doing in me, while I stood before the council," The witnesses note Paul's challenge to the present accusers. Barnes notes that since the Asian Jews were not present to witness about the temple, Paul calls on those present (the high priest and elders) to bear witness, if they can, to any improper conduct or violation of the law while he stood before the sanhedrim (Acts 23:1-10); a bold appeal showing his full consciousness of innocence; he admits they were competent to testify to his conduct there. The witnesses note Paul challenging the present accusers to name any wrongdoing they found in him before the council — the bold appeal of innocence, defying them to point to any fault. The witnesses note the challenge to the present accusers. The witnesses note the consciousness of innocence.

The witnesses note Paul challenging the present accusers to name any wrongdoing before the council — the bold appeal of innocence.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's challenge to the present accusers. The lesson the expositors draw is the bold appeal of innocence, defying the accusers to name any fault. The witnesses note the challenge: "let these same here say, if they have found any evil doing in me, while I stood before the council" — Paul calls on those present (the high priest and elders) to bear witness to any wrongdoing they found in him while he stood before the sanhedrim; Barnes notes a bold appeal; the challenge to the present accusers. The expositors observe the competence of these witnesses: Barnes notes Paul admits the present accusers were competent to testify to his conduct before the council (Acts 23:1-10), where they were present; the witnesses able to speak to that occasion; Paul appealing to their knowledge. The witnesses press the boldness of the appeal: Paul defies the accusers to name any "evil doing" they found in him before the council; the bold challenge; the full consciousness of innocence; the apostle unafraid; the defiance of the guiltless. The expositors draw the lesson on the consciousness of innocence: Barnes notes the appeal showed Paul's full consciousness of innocence; the apostle, sure of his integrity, challenges the accusers to find any fault; the clear conscience emboldening the challenge; the innocence confident. The witnesses observe the implied result: the accusers could name no real wrongdoing — only the matter of the resurrection (v.21), which was no crime; the challenge unanswerable; no fault to be found; the accusers silenced. The expositors note the wisdom of limiting the appeal: Paul appeals to the present accusers only for what they witnessed (before the council), and to the absent for the temple; the precise appeal to competent witnesses; the careful, just defence; the apostle's wisdom. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's challenge to the present accusers, the bold appeal of innocence, and the consciousness of integrity. The verse thus records Paul's challenge to the present accusers — let them name any evil doing — teaching the bold appeal of innocence, defying the accusers to name any fault.
21:
"Except it be for this one voice, that I cried standing among them, Touching the resurrection of the dead I am called in question by you this day." The witnesses note Paul's reference to the one cry about the resurrection. Gill notes Paul did not own this to be an evil in him, but that it was the only thing any of them — a part, the Sadducees — could judge to be evil; the resurrection being the one point. Barnes notes this was the main thing Paul had insisted on before the council; he calls them to show, if they could, that he was wrong in it; not a confession of wrong, but the one ground of their accusation. The witnesses note Paul granting that the only thing they could allege was his cry about the resurrection — no crime, but the true ground of the division and the accusation. The witnesses note the one voice. The witnesses note the resurrection the only charge.

The witnesses note Paul granting the only charge was his cry about the resurrection — no crime, the true ground of the accusation.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's reference to the one cry about the resurrection. The lesson the expositors draw is that the only ground of accusation was the resurrection, which was no crime but the true point at issue. The witnesses note the exception: "except it be for this one voice, that I cried standing among them, Touching the resurrection of the dead I am called in question" — Paul grants that the only thing the accusers could allege was his cry about the resurrection before the council (Acts 23:6); the one point; the single ground. The expositors observe Gill's note that Paul did not own this an evil: Paul did not confess this to be a wrong in him, but stated that it was the only thing any of them (a part, the Sadducees) could judge to be evil; not a confession of fault, but the naming of the one ground; the resurrection the only point. The witnesses press that the resurrection was no crime: Paul's cry about the resurrection was no crime — it was the orthodox hope (v.15), held by the Pharisees; the accusation reduced to a point of doctrine, not a crime; the charge dissolved into a theological question; no offence. The expositors draw the lesson on the true ground of the accusation: the real ground of the prosecution was not sedition or profaning the temple (which could not be proved), but the doctrine of the resurrection (and of Christ risen); the religious dispute the true issue; the political charges a pretext; the resurrection the heart of the matter; "of the hope and resurrection of the dead I am called in question" (Acts 23:6). The witnesses observe the division it exposed: the resurrection divided the Pharisees and Sadducees (Acts 23:6-9); Paul's cry exposed the division among his accusers; the question that split them; the charge resting on a disputed doctrine. The expositors note Barnes's point on the challenge: this was the main thing Paul had insisted on before the council; he calls the accusers to show, if they could, that he was wrong in it; the challenge to prove the resurrection a crime; the unanswerable point. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the one voice, the resurrection as the only charge, and the religious dispute as the true ground. The verse thus records Paul's reference to the one cry about the resurrection — teaching that the only ground of accusation was the resurrection, no crime but the true point at issue.
22:
"And when Felix heard these things, having more perfect knowledge of that way, he deferred them, and said, When Lysias the chief captain shall come down, I will know the uttermost of your matter." The witnesses note Felix's deferral of the case. Chrysostom observes Felix "put them off on purpose: not because he wanted to learn, but as wishing to get rid of the Jews; on their account he did not like to let him go; to punish him was not possible; that would have been too barefaced"; so entirely did he acquit Paul of the charges. The witnesses note Felix, having fuller knowledge of the Way, deferring the case until Lysias should come — the governor, convinced of Paul's innocence yet unwilling to offend the Jews, putting off judgment; the injustice of delay. The witnesses note the deferral. The witnesses note Felix's evasion.

The witnesses note Felix deferring the case — convinced of Paul's innocence yet unwilling to offend the Jews; the injustice of delay.

So the witnesses make the verse Felix's deferral of the case. The lesson the expositors draw is the injustice of delay, the governor convinced of Paul's innocence yet unwilling to acquit him for fear of the Jews. The witnesses note the deferral: "Felix... deferred them, and said, When Lysias the chief captain shall come down, I will know the uttermost of your matter" — Felix put off the case until Lysias should come; the postponement; the judgment deferred. The expositors observe Felix's fuller knowledge: "having more perfect knowledge of that way" — Felix knew more of the Christian Way (perhaps from his position, or his Jewish wife Drusilla, v.24); the governor not ignorant of the gospel; the knowledge that should have led to a just decision. The witnesses press Chrysostom's exposure of the evasion: Felix put them off on purpose — not because he wanted to learn, but to get rid of the Jews; on their account he did not like to let Paul go; to punish him was not possible (that would have been too barefaced); so entirely did he acquit Paul of the charges; the evasion that would neither condemn nor release. The expositors draw the lesson on the injustice of delay: Felix, convinced of Paul's innocence (he could not justly punish him), yet unwilling to offend the Jews by acquitting him, deferred the case; the injustice of the delay; the judge who would not do right for fear of men; the postponement of justice. The witnesses observe the fear of men in Felix: the governor, swayed by the fear of the Jews, would not acquit the innocent; the man-pleasing that perverts justice; the fear of man that delays the right; "the fear of man bringeth a snare" (Proverbs 29:25); the cowardice of the judge. The expositors note the pretext of waiting for Lysias: Felix's claim to await Lysias was a pretext; the captain's report (Acts 23:26-30) already cleared Paul; the deferral an evasion, not a genuine need for testimony; the excuse covering the injustice. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Felix's deferral, the conviction of Paul's innocence, and the injustice of delay for fear of the Jews. The verse thus records Felix's deferral of the case — until Lysias should come — teaching the injustice of delay, the governor unwilling to acquit the innocent for fear of the Jews.
23:
"And he commanded a centurion to keep Paul, and to let him have liberty, and that he should forbid none of his acquaintance to minister or come unto him." The witnesses note the mild custody granted to Paul. Barnes notes Felix was disposed to show Paul all the favours consistent with his safe keeping; he esteemed him a persecuted man, regarding the charges as malicious; perhaps detaining him to gratify the Jews, and hoping for money. Chrysostom notes that by giving him liberty and allowing his friends to minister, Felix so entirely acquitted him of the charges. The witnesses note Felix granting Paul liberty in custody, allowing his friends free access — the mild treatment that tacitly acknowledged his innocence, even as the unjust detention continued. The witnesses note the mild custody. The witnesses note the tacit acknowledgment of innocence.

The witnesses note the mild custody granted Paul — liberty and access to friends; the tacit acknowledgment of his innocence.

So the witnesses make the verse the mild custody granted to Paul. The lesson the expositors draw is the tacit acknowledgment of Paul's innocence in the mild treatment, even as the unjust detention continued. The witnesses note the liberty: "he commanded a centurion to keep Paul, and to let him have liberty" — Felix ordered Paul kept in custody, yet with liberty; the mild confinement; not close imprisonment, but a relaxed custody; the favour shown. The expositors observe the access to friends: "should forbid none of his acquaintance to minister or come unto him" — Paul's friends were allowed free access to minister to him; the liberty to receive his friends; the mild treatment; the apostle not cut off. The witnesses press the tacit acknowledgment of innocence: Barnes notes Felix esteemed Paul a persecuted man, regarding the charges as malicious; Chrysostom notes that by granting liberty and access, Felix so entirely acquitted him of the charges; the mild treatment tacitly acknowledging his innocence; the favour confessing the case had no merit. The expositors draw the lesson on the inconsistency of Felix: Felix treated Paul mildly (esteeming him innocent), yet detained him (to please the Jews); the inconsistency of the unjust judge — acknowledging innocence yet withholding justice; the half-measure; the conscience and the cowardice together. The witnesses observe the providence in the liberty: the liberty allowed Paul's friends to minister to him, and Paul to continue some ministry; the providence softening the confinement; the apostle comforted by his friends; the mercy in the custody. The expositors note the mixed motives of Felix that Barnes marks: Felix detained Paul perhaps to gratify the Jews (keeping him as if guilty), and hoping for money (v.26); the mixed motives; the favour and the injustice together; the self-interest in the custody. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the mild custody, the access to friends, and the tacit acknowledgment of Paul's innocence. The verse thus records the mild custody granted Paul — liberty and access to friends — teaching the tacit acknowledgment of his innocence, even as the unjust detention continued.
24:
"And after certain days, when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, which was a Jewess, he sent for Paul, and heard him concerning the faith in Christ." The witnesses note Felix and Drusilla sending for Paul to hear the gospel. Gill and Barnes note Drusilla was the daughter of Herod Agrippa the elder, a Jewess, whom Felix had lured from her husband Azizus to marry him — their union itself adulterous. The witnesses note Felix, with his wife Drusilla (a Jewess, taken unlawfully), sending for Paul to hear him concerning the faith in Christ — the curiosity that brought the corrupt pair to hear the gospel; the apostle given opportunity to preach Christ to the rulers. The witnesses note the sending for Paul. The witnesses note the hearing concerning the faith.

The witnesses note Felix and Drusilla sending for Paul to hear the faith in Christ — the apostle given opportunity to preach to the rulers.

So the witnesses make the verse Felix and Drusilla hearing Paul. The lesson the expositors draw is the opportunity given Paul to preach Christ to the rulers, and the corrupt pair who heard. The witnesses note the occasion: "Felix came with his wife Drusilla, which was a Jewess, he sent for Paul, and heard him concerning the faith in Christ" — Felix, with Drusilla, sent for Paul to hear him concerning the faith in Christ; the curiosity that brought them to hear; the apostle summoned to preach. The expositors observe the character of Drusilla: Gill and Barnes note Drusilla was the daughter of Herod Agrippa the elder, a Jewess; Felix had lured her from her husband Azizus (king of Emesa) to marry him; their union itself adulterous; the corrupt pair; the unlawful marriage. The witnesses press the opportunity to preach Christ: Paul was given the opportunity to preach the faith in Christ to the governor and his wife; the apostle preaching to the rulers; the gospel brought before the great; the door opened; the prisoner the preacher. The expositors draw the lesson on the opportunity in the trial: Paul's imprisonment gave him the opportunity to preach Christ to Felix and Drusilla; the providence turning the confinement to a pulpit; the apostle witnessing to the rulers; "before governors and kings for my sake, for a testimony" (Matthew 10:18); the opportunity in the bonds. The witnesses observe the curiosity that brought them: Felix and Drusilla sent for Paul perhaps from curiosity (Drusilla a Jewess, interested in the Way; Felix knowing of the gospel, v.22); the curiosity that brought them to hear; the hearing not yet faith; the curious audience. The expositors note the faithfulness Paul would show: Paul, given this opportunity, would not flatter the corrupt pair, but reason of righteousness, temperance, and judgment (v.25); the faithful preaching to come; the apostle preaching the searching truth; the faithfulness to the rulers. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Felix and Drusilla sending for Paul, the opportunity to preach Christ, and the corrupt pair who heard. The verse thus records Felix and Drusilla hearing Paul concerning the faith — teaching the opportunity given Paul to preach Christ to the rulers.
25:
"And as he reasoned of righteousness, temperance, and judgment to come, Felix trembled, and answered, Go thy way for this time; when I have a convenient season, I will call for thee." The witnesses note Paul's searching sermon and Felix's trembling postponement. Gill notes Paul reasoned of righteousness (the doing of justice between man and man — a virtue greatly wanting in the cruel and unjust Felix), of temperance (against his adulterous union with Drusilla), and of judgment to come; falling pointedly on the sins of his hearers. The witnesses note Paul reasoning of righteousness, temperance, and judgment to come — the truths that struck at Felix's injustice and adultery — and Felix, trembling under conviction, putting it off to "a convenient season"; the famous example of conviction resisted and postponed. The witnesses note the searching sermon. The witnesses note the postponed conviction.

The witnesses note Paul's searching sermon and Felix trembling, postponing to "a convenient season" — conviction resisted and deferred.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's searching sermon and Felix's trembling postponement. The lesson the expositors draw is the faithful, searching preaching that convicts, and the tragedy of conviction resisted and deferred. The witnesses note the searching themes: "as he reasoned of righteousness, temperance, and judgment to come" — Paul reasoned of righteousness (justice between man and man — Gill notes a virtue greatly wanting in the cruel, unjust Felix), of temperance (self-control — striking at his adulterous union with Drusilla), and of judgment to come (the reckoning before God); the truths chosen to strike at the sins of his hearers. The expositors observe the faithfulness of the preaching: Paul, before the corrupt governor and his unlawful wife, did not flatter, but reasoned of the very truths that condemned them — righteousness (against Felix's injustice), temperance (against his adultery), judgment (the coming reckoning); the faithful, searching preaching; the apostle fearless before the great; the truth pressed home. The witnesses press Felix's trembling: "Felix trembled" — the governor, convicted by the searching truth, trembled; the conviction of conscience; the powerful man shaken before the prisoner's word; the conscience pierced; the trembling of conviction. The expositors draw the lesson on the tragedy of the postponement: "Go thy way for this time; when I have a convenient season, I will call for thee" — Felix, trembling under conviction, yet put off the decision to "a convenient season"; the conviction resisted; the deferral of repentance; the famous, fatal postponement; "the convenient season" that never came; the tragedy of delay.
