The witnesses observe the danger of the postponed conviction: Felix trembled, but did not repent; he put off the decision, and (so far as we know) the convenient season never came; the conviction resisted hardening into impenitence; "to day if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts" (Hebrews 3:15); the peril of delay. The expositors note the warning to all: Felix's trembling and postponement a warning — the danger of resisting conviction, of putting off repentance to a convenient season; the season that may never come; the conviction grieved away; the warning against delay; "now is the accepted time" (2 Corinthians 6:2). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the searching sermon, Felix's trembling, and the tragedy of conviction postponed. The verse thus records Paul's searching sermon and Felix's trembling postponement — go thy way for this time — teaching the faithful preaching that convicts and the tragedy of conviction resisted and deferred.
26:
"He hoped also that money should have been given him of Paul, that he might loose him: wherefore he sent for him the oftener, and communed with him." The witnesses note Felix's hope of a bribe. Gill notes Felix observed from Paul's defence that he had come to bring alms, and from the indictment that he was a man of some consequence with a large following; he hoped Paul or his friends would supply money to obtain his freedom; perhaps this was why he allowed free access. Barnes notes the hope to weary Paul into purchasing his freedom. The witnesses note Felix, hoping for a bribe, sending for Paul often — the corruption of the judge, who, having trembled under the word, now sought gain from the prisoner; the covetousness that perverted justice. The witnesses note the hope of a bribe. The witnesses note the corruption of Felix.

The witnesses note Felix hoping for a bribe, sending for Paul often — the corruption of the judge, covetousness perverting justice.

So the witnesses make the verse Felix's hope of a bribe. The lesson the expositors draw is the corruption of the judge, covetousness perverting justice. The witnesses note the hope: "he hoped also that money should have been given him of Paul, that he might loose him" — Felix hoped Paul would offer money to obtain his freedom; Gill notes Felix observed Paul had come to bring alms, and was a man of some consequence with a following, so hoped he or his friends would supply money; the hope of a bribe. The expositors observe the corruption this reveals: Felix, who had trembled under the word (v.25), now sought gain from the prisoner; the covetousness of the judge; the corruption that would sell justice; the bribe sought; the venal governor. The witnesses press the frequent interviews: "wherefore he sent for him the oftener, and communed with him" — Felix sent for Paul often, hoping for the bribe; the frequent interviews driven by greed, not by concern for the truth; the corrupt motive; the conversations for gain. The expositors draw the lesson on the covetousness that perverts justice: Felix, hoping for money, kept Paul in custody; the justice perverted by greed; the judge who would sell freedom for a bribe; "the love of money is the root of all evil" (1 Timothy 6:10); the covetousness corrupting the judge. The witnesses observe the contrast with Paul's integrity: Paul, who would not buy his freedom with a bribe, kept his integrity though it cost him his liberty; the apostle's honesty against the judge's greed; the integrity that would not corrupt; the contrast of the corrupt judge and the upright prisoner. The expositors note the hardening after the trembling: Felix trembled (v.25), but instead of repenting, turned to seeking a bribe; the conviction not followed by repentance, but by a return to covetousness; the conscience grieved, the heart hardened; the trembling followed by greed; the tragedy continued. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Felix's hope of a bribe, the frequent interviews, and the corruption that perverted justice. The verse thus records Felix's hope of a bribe — sending for Paul often — teaching the corruption of the judge, covetousness perverting justice.
27:
"But after two years Porcius Festus came into Felix' room: and Felix, willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, left Paul bound." The witnesses note Felix leaving Paul bound to please the Jews. Barnes notes Paul was unjustly detained two years; Felix's hope was to weary him into purchasing his freedom; "Felix, willing to shew the Jews a pleasure" — desirous of pleasing them, even at the expense of justice; he left Paul bound to conciliate the Jews and prevent them accusing him for the evils of his administration. Gill notes the two years were of Paul's confinement at Caesarea. The witnesses note Felix, recalled after two years, leaving Paul still bound to gratify the Jews — the final injustice of the man-pleasing judge, sacrificing the innocent to win favour. The witnesses note the two years. The witnesses note the injustice for favour.

The witnesses note Felix leaving Paul bound to please the Jews — the final injustice of the man-pleasing judge, sacrificing the innocent.

So the witnesses make the verse Felix leaving Paul bound to please the Jews. The lesson the expositors draw is the final injustice of the man-pleasing judge, sacrificing the innocent to win favour. The witnesses note the two years: "after two years Porcius Festus came into Felix' room" — after two years, Felix was succeeded by Festus; Gill notes the two years were of Paul's confinement at Caesarea; Barnes notes Paul was unjustly detained all this time; the long, unjust imprisonment. The expositors observe the injustice of the detention: Paul, innocent (as Felix knew), was detained two years; the prolonged injustice; the apostle held without cause; the unjust confinement; the years lost to imprisonment. The witnesses press the man-pleasing motive: "Felix, willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, left Paul bound" — Felix, to please the Jews, left Paul in bonds; Barnes notes he was desirous of pleasing them, even at the expense of justice, to conciliate them and prevent their accusing him for the evils of his administration; the man-pleasing that sacrificed justice. The expositors draw the lesson on the injustice for favour: Felix sacrificed the innocent Paul to win the favour of the Jews; the justice perverted to please men; the judge who would do wrong to gain favour; "they loved the praise of men more than the praise of God" (John 12:43); the man-pleasing injustice. The witnesses observe the self-interest in the act: Felix left Paul bound to conciliate the Jews, lest they accuse him before the emperor for his misgovernment; the self-interest mingled with the man-pleasing; the judge protecting himself at the cost of the innocent; the corrupt calculation. The expositors note the providence in the two years: though unjustly detained, Paul was preserved; the providence using the time (perhaps for the writing, the ministry to visitors, the rest); the apostle kept for further service (the journey to Rome, Acts 27); the providence over the injustice; the time not wasted in God's purpose. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the two years, Felix leaving Paul bound, and the injustice for favour. The verse thus records Felix leaving Paul bound to please the Jews — teaching the final injustice of the man-pleasing judge, sacrificing the innocent to win favour, and the providence preserving Paul through the unjust detention.
